data.title: Calamos Global Convertible Fund: Positioned For Curveballs
data.description:
By Eli Pars, CFA

For Q1 2026, Global convertibles once again performed well compared to equities, delivering a 2.0% gain as measured by the FTSE Global Convertible Bond Index, while the MSCI World Index was down -3.5%. The equities underlying the global convertible index also held up better than the global equity market, declining only -0.84%.

Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) moved around with the market during Q1 2026 but finished basically flat, posting a return of 0.50% at quarter end. This is sometimes referred to on Wall Street as “aggressively unchanged.”

As we have discussed in our past commentaries, our goal is lower-volatility participation in the global equity market over the long term. We position the portfolio with the aim of capturing the majority of equity upside while maintaining downside resilience.

We believe our dual focus on achieving returns and mitigating risk will serve the fund. Last quarter, we talked about how “geopolitics can add plenty of curveballs.” Well, that ended up being quite an understatement. But the market is holding up well, in our view. From the start of the Middle East war on February 28 through the end of the quarter, the S&P 500 Index is down just under 5%. On January 1, 2026, the S&P 500 was up 86% cumulatively over the past three years. Considering that, a 5% correction seems pretty tame to us.

We see many of the same tailwinds supporting economic growth as we saw at the start of the year: short-term rates more likely to move lower than higher and a global construction boom driven by the AI data center buildout. However, until we get some resolution in Iran, it is difficult to get aggressive. We’ll continue to lean into convertibles’ downside risk mitigation.

One of the challenges in the global convertible market today is the combination of its narrowness and the violent upside movement of some of the winners, which have risen 5x to 10x within a couple of years. This handful of names is dominated by tech but also includes what we consider a rare creature: a fast-growing European conglomerate. We’ve taken a deliberate approach that seeks to participate in the hottest growth industries without sacrificing the risk mitigation that convertible structures ideally can offer. However, regardless of sector, it’s tough to own converts when they are deep in the money (call it a winner’s curse). But as the stocks continue to work, it’s also tough not owning those converts. Managing position sizes is a balancing act.

Positioning

The fund’s largest allocation continues to be to technology issues. Technology is an area that offers excellent opportunities for our active approach. The universe of convertible tech issuers includes many high-growth opportunities, including AI beneficiaries, and the convertible structure helps dampen volatility. Given the appreciation we’ve seen in many of these names and the increased equity sensitivity, we are closely managing position sizes in this area.

From a regional standpoint, we continue to find the most opportunities in the United States. The fund maintains its underweight to Europe based on the macro environment and bottom-up considerations. As we have discussed, we see a tax and regulatory environment in the region that creates headwinds to company growth. Additionally, Europe’s investor base tends to favor defensive structures, while we prefer securities with a better balance between upside opportunity and downside risk management. That said, we have found opportunities to invest in synthetic convertibles that offer the risk-management and reward characteristics we seek.

Global Convertible Issuance: Hot out of the Gate in 2026

Global convertible issuance for the first quarter is off to a strong start, with $53.3 billion coming to market—more than twice as much as the first quarter of 2025. We saw the strongest issuance in February, and although geopolitics dampened issuance in March, activity was still very strong. For the quarter, US companies led with $27.0 billion, followed by Asia ($14.5 billion) and Europe ($7.6 billion). Japan rounded out the list at $4.2 billion, which is more than double its total issuance for all of 2025.

We see a positive setup for issuance from here, but the bar is high to match the run rate seen in Q2–Q4 of 2025. It’s also important to remember that issuance ebbs and flows due to a variety of factors. Historically, the first month of a quarter tends to see more subdued issuance because of earnings announcements.

Convertible securities vary in their levels of equity and fixed income sensitivity. The most equity-sensitive convertibles offer higher levels of upside but also greater exposure to downside. In contrast, more pronounced fixed income characteristics provide a greater cushion against downside in exchange for less upside opportunity.

The Boom in New Global Convertible Issuance Continued in Q1 2026

Total Return % as of 3/31/26 1Q26 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year 10 Year Fund Inception
Calamos Global Convertible Fund (CXGCX) 0.50 17.02 12.58 3.02 8.05 6.93 (12/31/14)
FTSE Global Convertibles Index 2.00 24.56 13.98 4.72 8.42 7.25

Source: Morningstar. Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Please refer to important Risk Information. The principal value and return of an investment will fluctuate so that your shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. The funds’ gross expense ratio as of the prospectus dated 2/27/2026 is 1.08% for Class I Shares.

Original Post

data.title: OXLC Is A Mess - Here's Why I'm Bullish
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Oxford Lane (OXLC) shares are up 20% in recent weeks after finding some support near the $8/share level. The stock price experienced steep declines in the past three months after write-offs, and the weakening portfolio hit its NAV/share.

In the previous article, I highlighted a potential market overreaction as the price discount to NAV surged. However, this opportunity should be viewed within the context of elevated risk. Although buying OXLC at a discount to NAV improves the chances of achieving positive total returns, those chances aren't particularly high to begin with. A significant portion of investors sit at a loss despite holding OXLC for many years. A better alternative is the company's notes and preferreds, such as OXLCG, the main focus of this piece.

Capital Losses

OXLC suffers from structural issues that make NAV/share declines inevitable. The company is required to distribute nearly all its ordinary income (interest and dividend income from its CLO portfolio). It is required to make these distributions regardless of capital gains/losses, which have their own distribution test. In theory, capital losses would be offset by capital gains. In reality, it's mostly losses. The reasons a leveraged SME is likely to experience a credit deterioration instead of a credit upgrade are beyond the scope of this piece. For the purpose of this article, it is enough to note that this has historically been the case for OXLC.

Since 2015, the company has written off roughly $900 million in assets at a rate of $75 million annually, a reflection of its ice-cube business model that emphasizes total returns as opposed to capital preservation.

OXLC's business model shifts the credit risk of its loan portfolio to its shareholders instead of managing that risk internally. The monthly dividend decisions are based on cash income, with capital losses (write-offs, defaults within CLO, etc.) treated as an operational issue. The business model is shaped by tax laws as much as economic considerations. As a registered investment company (RIC), OXLC enjoys enormous tax benefits. At the same time, it is required to distribute nearly all its ordinary income, leaving almost nothing to build a loan loss reserve when things go south.

Net Income and Profitability

Two conditions are necessary to successfully invest in subprime loans and derivatives

  • Tolerance for risk
  • Adequate yield to compensate for losses

For OXLC, the question is whether the CLOs generate enough yield to compensate for

  1. Capital Losses
  2. Operating Costs
  3. Financing Costs

One metric that captures all these factors is the "Net Increase in Net Assets Resulting from Operations." It is the equivalent of the "net income".

Using it to gauge the profitability of the core business yields mixed results. Since 2015, "net income" has been inconsistent, with years of steep losses followed by gains. On average, "Net Income" is modest, standing at about $25 million annually.

Most critically, it might not reflect the true performance. OXLC continuously raises equity capital. CLO performance is typically strongest in earlier days, with deterioration being gradual as time passes. Raising capital does artificially boost performance. More importantly, it increases dilution. Although management emphasizes looking at cash income, which it says is the basis of its dividend distribution decisions, it still reports NAV declines.

The Odds: What Holding OXLC for 1 Year Is Like

Investors buying OXLC's common shares are betting that the dividend income will offset the inevitable NAV/share decline by a margin enough to leave a decent yield. Historically, the odds of this happening aren't particularly great. The following analysis assumes an investor buys and holds OXLC for one year.

In this scenario, there is no need to annualize the return because it is a 1-year rolling total return calculation.

The chart below shows that holding OXLC for one year isn't a guarantee for positive returns.

Technical note: The chart excludes holding periods of less than one year. Thus, the cut-off date is January 1st, 2025.

The median return is 6%, the average return is 8%, and the standard deviation is 33%. This means that a typical investor holding OXLC common shares will earn less than an investor holding its fixed-income securities, such as OXLCG.

It is worth noting that the 75th percentile of the observed 1-year rolling returns is ~20%. This means that 25% of observations had a return above 20%. This chance to lock in extraordinary returns contributes to supporting the business model, which depends on continuous capital raises.

OXLGC

Oxford Lane 7.95% Notes due 2031 offer a better risk/reward alternative to holding the common shares.

The chart above shows that the common shares failed to generate annual returns above its 2031 Notes more than half of the time. Only 47% of the observations have a yield above 8%, which is what OXLCG currently yields. OXLC common shareholders aren't being properly compensated for the risk.

Moreover, OXLCG offers a clean exit, with notes maturing in 2031, assuaging the NAV/share decline dynamics impacting the common shareholders. OXLCG now trades below redemption price for the first time in many months, as liquidity evaporates and the risk-off trade intensifies on the back of escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

Finally, OXLC isn't highly leveraged, with $2.3 billion in assets and $802 billion in liabilities as of December 2025 (p.S-20). In H1'26, interest expense stood at $27 million (or $54 million annualized) (p.31). This is a small figure for a portfolio of high-yield assets measured in billions. For perspective, OXLC generated $109 million in net core NII in Q3'26 alone (three months ended December 2025).

Final Thoughts and Risks

OXLC might not be leveraged on the corporate level, but its CLOs - the underlying portfolio companies - are leveraged SMEs. There are signs of stress in the private equity market, which could have spillover effects on OXLC's underlying CLO loans. A lack of liquidity might have a very detrimental effect on OXLC's common shares. This article carries a speculative buy rating on OXLC common shares because the market is already pricing a significant discount to NAV. The February investor update puts NAV/share between $11.59/share and $11.89/share, a relatively steep discount to the current price of $9.86/share.

The NAV/share declines are part of OXLC's business model. The company determines distributions more based on cash proceeds instead of GAAP "Net Income". The mandatory distribution requirements mean that the company can't build a loan loss reserve. Investors' bet is that the monthly distributions would offset an inevitable decline in NAV/share and share price. Based on the past 1-year rolling returns, the odds of investors earning high total returns aren't particularly great.

The better alternative is OXLC's public notes and preferreds. This article focuses on OXLCG, the company's senior notes maturing in 2031. They offer a smaller but more consistent yield. The notes now trade below redemption price for the first time in many months and currently have a floating yield of 8%. As long as OXLC has access to capital markets, I believe that the model is sustainable.

data.title: U.S. Teens See Gains As Total U.S. Jobs Declines In March 2026
data.description:
According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics' latest jobs data, the number of employed Americans fell by a seasonally-adjusted 64,000 since February, dropping to 162,848,000 in March 2026.

Normally, that situation wouldn't bode well for U.S. teens, but the same employment situation report indicates the number of Age 16 to 19-year-olds counted as having jobs rose by 68,000 from February to March.

Both younger teens (Age 16-17) and older teens (Age 18-19) saw gains. The number of younger teens increased by 37,000 to a seasonally-adjusted 2,000,000 and the number of working older teens grew by 41,000 to 3,459,000.

The following pair of charts presents the seasonally-adjusted data for both the number of teens employed in each of these age categories and also the U.S. teen employment-to-population ratio for the period from January 2021 through March 2026.

Since the total number of seasonally-adjusted jobs fell while the number of working teens increased, that means the number of Americans Age 20 or older had to fall. Which it did, dropping 132,000 to a seasonally-adjusted 157,423,000 in March 2026.

Note that for this jobs data, each demographic gets its own seasonal adjustment, which means this data won't necessarily add up to the seasonally adjusted total for the combined population. If you want numbers that do add up, you'll want to extract the non-seasonally adjusted data from the BLS' labor force statistics database.

Overall, the March 2026 employment situation data is a little strange. Perhaps its strangeness is attributable to run-of-the-mill sampling variation, but we'll know if there's something more behind it over the next few months.

References

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Labor Force Statistics (Current Population Survey - CPS). [Online Database]. Accessed: 2 April 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bowman Consulting: Capturing Utility Growth Without Paying A Premium
data.description:
In my last article, I wanted to explain the global electrification boom in recent years. The article was about American Electric Power (AEP), which seemed to be a very interesting option to participate in the mentioned boom, thanks to its powerful energy infrastructure based on coal and large investments in transmission. If you have some stock in AEP, I recommend keeping it. But I wouldn't add to my position, at least for now.

AEP has a market cap of $71 billion, so it is a high-quality option. These companies are very analyzed by the market that has already priced in their potential. That's why, sometimes, the best opportunities are in companies of small and mid capitalization. In this case, I see that opportunity in Bowman Consulting (BWMN), an energy infrastructure construction company that is getting positioned in the electrification boom.

With a market cap of $470 million, the company doesn't appear to have been viewed by the market as an attractive investment in Utilities (XLU). And that's because BWMN operates in four segments, each one with significant internal diversification. In the same way it builds streets, houses, and offices, it also provides full-service solutions for power generation and data centers. Its low market cap also has some risks, such as a higher sensitivity to interest rates, but I believe the cost-benefit analysis is favorable. Besides, the company is trading below its peers while its backlog continues to grow.

The electrification boom

I want to go back just for a moment to the electrification boom. I believe the Middle East conflict will, once again, demonstrate the need for energy diversification. Yes, it's not only an energy transition. I do believe the world needs more diversification. I would even say that greater energy diversification accelerates the transition to renewable sources.

On the way to transition, I believe that to diversify is also to use non-renewable sources. It's not only oil but also coal. It also means accepting that nuclear energy is not only useful but safe. Even more: Analysts estimate nuclear energy will go from 33 TWh to 65 TWh by 2030. The world will, indeed, need more energy. Maybe more than ever before. The middle class is growing, and new technologies need more electricity: EVs and charging stations, heating replacements, consumer electronics, and AI data centers. Until 2030, electricity demand will grow 3.6% annually, according to the IEA.

Industry and the residential sector will continue to be the big drivers of demand, with strong growth in data centers. This goes in line with the uses I mentioned before: electricity replacing gas in both industries and buildings, and AI revolutionizing the world of data centers.

This electrification boom will need very strong investments in energy infrastructure, besides generation. Investment in electricity networks is estimated to increase from $390 billion in 2024 to approximately $600 billion in 2030. The largest expenditures require infrastructure improvements. For example, the expansion of the network to reach places that were not previously thought to be reachable, the use of intelligent systems to maintain affordability, or making the transmission system stronger to withstand higher voltage.

The barrier to entry in a costly business led me to Bowman Consulting

Besides generation sources, the infrastructure that will sustain energy is what I like to invest in. And here comes Bowman Consulting. Generators typically also participate in transmission (where electricity travels at its highest voltage) and in distribution (where lower loads travel to reach the residential sector).

I believe this is something logical, because its operations are yet so robust that they could offer better services than a newly arrived player. For example, American Electric Power has planned an investment of $72 billion through 2030, with assets already valued at $114 billion (slide 111 of 10-K). Entering into the electrification business (generation, transmission, and distribution, mainly) is too expensive.

However, the engineering, design, and logistics of network construction, along with data center services, are a more specialized job. Bowman does that job and is increasing its exposure to utilities more and more. And utilities are very important because they are the sector that contributes most to energy resilience. Behind them are the industrial companies that are dedicated to the manufacture of equipment and related constructions.

Utility exposure in Bowman's portfolio

It is in this context that I'm paying attention to Bowman. Its backlog in utilities increased from 13% in 2022 to 24% in 2025. In the following chart, you can see the bigger weight in the portfolio, marked in yellow.

I believe the decrease in weight in building and transport infrastructure is clear. I also see good performance in natural resources, with a significant weight in the advance of mining and infrastructure services in water systems.

With its backlog declining, Building Infrastructure is facing a weak real estate market, with low demand and low affordability in multi-family housing. Mortgage rates above 6% for 30-year loans do not help the segment. While industrial offices are facing another low demand crisis since the pandemic. In this case, the increase of home offices was a very important downward catalyst. Building infrastructure still has a strong weight in revenues, representing 42% of the total. It is also the segment with the best gross margin: 55.8%.

But as compensation for the weaker momentum in futures contracts, the greater exposure to utilities offers significant opportunities. The backlog is just a sample of that. The power, utilities, and energy sector represented 23% of income in 2025, while its gross margin was 55.3%, just 50 bps below Building Infrastructure.

Within the revenues from utilities, traditional energy represents 14%, being the most significant. Data centers, at the closing of Q4 2025, represented 6%, when two quarters ago it was 4%. For Bowman, the data centers business offers a lot of possibilities, and I am waiting for it to keep growing in the following quarters. McKinsey estimates an investment necessary in data centers of $1.3 trillion through 2030. For a company with such a small capitalization, it represents a significant market opportunity. Besides, Bowman takes advantage of the utilization of several services thanks to its logistical knowledge. The multi-service advantage was something I already suspected when researching the company. But I found a better explanation given by the COO of the company, Daniel Swayze:

Data center programs are rarely single service projects. They are multiphase, multiservice opportunities. For example, combining the electrical and mechanical engineering forces from our e3i acquisition, the fire and life safety design services from our Fisher acquisition with our established capabilities in civil planning and engineering, we have a strong service offering our clients can rely on.

Conference call

Q4 2025: Revenues confirm a growing business

Before seeing the public services catalyst and the electrification boom (along with the demand for AI data centers), Bowman is already showing a business that delivers very good results. And I believe one of the main characteristics is the diversification of sectors in each segment. This means that no single segment has too much weight on revenue. And when one gets weaker (such as building infrastructure in the last years), others grow, such as natural resources and utilities.

That combination of sectors shows an incredible revenue increase from $261 million in 2022 to $420 million in 2025. Revenues for Q1 2026 are expected to be between $496 and $510 million. But it's not only revenues, because margins play a key role in companies that offer infrastructure services. And margins are growing too. Adjusted EBITDA margin went from 14.2% in 2022 to 16.8% in 2025. For 2026, the company estimates an adjusted EBITDA margin between 17% and 17.5%.

The $490 million for 2025 exceeded the upper range of their estimate by $10 million. However, both earnings per share and revenues of Q4 didn't surpass the consensus estimate. GAAP EPS fell short of estimates by $0.13, while revenues were $152k below expectations. I believe the market sometimes over-punishes some quarterly misses, and that was part of the drop in the price of its shares in recent months. But in 2025, looking at the full picture, I believe it was a very good year.

Finally, what I see in a company with a low market cap is its cash generation. Even more so when it is dedicated to a sector that uses a lot of capital, such as infrastructure and logistics. The FCF conversion was 46% in 2025 versus 40% in 2024. I mean, from the $73 million of adjusted EBITDA, $33.4 million was cash generation. Added to that is net debt of $178 million and a leverage ratio of 2.45x, which they expect to lower to 2x in 2026. They also have $154 million available from a line of credit. In summary, I see a solid financial position in a sector with high capital intensity and, most importantly, strong cash generation during these uncertain times with high interest rates.

Attractive valuation in an expensive sector

As I said at the beginning, Bowman is trading at some discounts due to the recent drop in its share price. In the last six months, it fell more than 30%, while so far this year, it has dropped 15%. Given its past strong growth and continued solid growth prospects, PEG FWD of 0.9x is almost 40% below the industry median.

Furthermore, expected EV/EBITDA is 8.3x versus the industry median of 11.4x, a difference of approximately 27%. To make a comparison with other similar companies, I looked at other metrics of Orion Group Holdings (ORN), which has a similar market cap. ORN has an EV/EBITDA FWD of 8.9x but trades at 181x its earnings per share, while BWMN trades at 39x. Another similar company is Concrete Pumping (BBCP), and its P/E is 73x. BWMN may appear expensive on a P/E basis, but compared to its peers, it trades at a reasonable valuation. Besides, as I've said before, the exposure of BWMN to utilities could cause an expensive valuation. But that isn't happening. If I compare it with Quanta Services (PWR), the biggest company in the sector, the difference gets even bigger. PWR has an EV/EBITDA FWD of 25x and trades at 80 times its earnings.

In summary, Bowman still has cheap prices in a sector like utilities that is usually expensive. Its exposure to utilities today is about a quarter of its operations, but it could be more in the future. If I consider only the difference between the EV/EBITDA of 8.3x versus the sector average of 11.4x, the undervaluation is 27%. Applied to the current price, I estimate a target price between $34 and $37 based on the growth potential for 2026.

Risks

Part of the risk of investing in Bowman is that its capacity might not be enough to supply the demand of the electrification boom. For the bigger companies of the sector, the risk, I believe, is not being able to quickly monetize the investment in the mid-term, although in some cases it far exceeds its market cap. Bowman is protected from that risk but is exposed to another: the challenge of scaling its capacity fast enough to meet growing demand.

Bowman has no responsibility in generation, transmission, or distribution, but it could lose ground in a highly competitive market. For example, Quanta Services (PWR) has a higher exposure to electric infrastructure due to its bigger size (market cap: $82 billion). I don't think it's a fair comparison with Bowman because they are different services, but it can compete for the same customers in the power, utilities, and energy segment. Despite those risks, Bowman has an advantage due to, precisely, its diversification, and that's why it is a very attractive investment for those looking for a more moderate exposure to the electrification boom. My thesis is that exposure will increase, and it will be reflected in the share price.

Conclusions

The electrification boom, catalyzed by AI data centers, I believe represents a long-term investment opportunity. Unfortunately, I believe the market got ahead of that boom in 2025 and priced at a premium valuation for the utilities sector. But Bowman is a hidden infrastructure company that seems to have gone unnoticed.

Its financial strength, revenue growth, and growing backlog are driving the company's increasing exposure to utilities. I believe the market hasn't priced in the fact that Bowman can become a key part of the chain value of AI data centers. While hyperscalers invest billions in data centers, the big generators and ex-crypto miners are gaining prominence. Behind those giants appears Bowman, which is trading at attractive valuations, representing a unique opportunity in a typically expensive sector.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters: Moving Back To Hold Rating As There Are Encouraging Developments (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) in October 2025, for which I recommended a sell rating as I thought the valuation was too stretched relative to the underlying fundamentals, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In this update, I still see some of those issues, especially within the AE brand and around tariffs. But the recent performance was clearly better, led by strong Aerie momentum and some signs of improvement at AE. In my view, that weakens the bear case enough for me to move back to a hold rating.

What got better

My original bearish view was that AEO’s recovery was mostly bought through promotions and that its pricing power was weak; margin weakness was also visible, and the setup did not look like a sustainable turnaround. I also thought that Aerie had shown some green shoots, but it is not enough to move the needle, and the American Eagle [AE] brand itself still was not showing the kind of sales acceleration one would expect after all the noise around the celebrity campaigns. Looking at the recent developments, I’d say there is evidence to suggest my bearish view has weakened. I am not ready to turn bullish yet, but I also do not think the right stance is still bearish.

One of the key developments here is Aerie. Previously, I said Aerie was not going to move the needle because it was only up 3%, but I am clearly wrong here. Aerie’s comp growth accelerated significantly to 23% in Q4 2025, with new Aerie customers up 14% and brand awareness up 12% y/y. This wasn’t “bought” through celebrity campaigns but appears to be real organic demand, as it was broad-based across categories and channels. With this level of growth, Aerie is a game changer, especially as it is 40% of total revenue and the largest mix of adj. EBIT. This becomes an even more important growth anchor vs. AE, and there is now a strong angle that growth could potentially accelerate if this momentum continues.

As for the AE banner, we also finally have proof that it can move in the right direction. I previously noted that all the attention around Sydney Sweeney did not really translate into meaningful AE brand sales, which was fair based on what the comp sales performance showed (down 3%). It seems like the campaign had a delayed impact, as comp sales came in at 2% in the recent quarter. While that is still not a huge number, there are encouraging underlying improvements. For instance, men posted a third consecutive quarter of growth with strength across nearly every men’s category. It is still hard to say for certain that AE has turned the corner, but I think the evidence suggests it is improving, and it does weaken a key part of my bearish view.

Why I am staying neutral

The last part about AE brings me to why I am staying neutral. The main issue is that AE is improving, but it is not fully fixed. As much as the men's category did well, I was still discouraged by the fact that management said AE women were flat in the quarter. I would expect women to do well since the Sydney Sweeney campaign was, I believe, for women. Moreover, AE's average unit retail was down, and management said jeans were promoted more deeply to stay competitive. The way I perceive all this is that some sales improvement is still being driven by discounting rather than stronger full-price demand, and that is a key issue. At the end of the day, organic demand is what drives long-term value, and if AE cannot capture that, I don’t think the market is going to price in a clean recovery. For a start, AE needs to show that the women’s business can improve and the full-price sales mix can go up.

Tariffs are another reason I cannot turn bullish. Management said the net tariff impact was ~$50 million in Q4, and 1H2026 is still seeing some pressure, with ~$30 million of tariff headwind expected in each of Q1 and Q2. I get that AEO is planning for mitigation through actions like better sourcing, operational efficiencies, and lower freight, but still, the earnings risk is very real. If consumer demand softens even a bit, or if the mitigation effort is less effective than expected, this tariff impact could potentially wipe a substantial chunk off the earnings base. For reference, FY25 adjusted earnings were $270 million, so say that AEO mitigation efforts fail, and the net tariff impact is, say, closer to $80 million (my guess); it represents about 30% of the FY25 adjusted earnings base. I am not saying this will knock off earnings by that much in FY26, but it is a risk to be recognized.

Valuation

Apart from the improvements recently, an important reason I am upgrading back to hold is that AEO’s valuation (NTM P/E) is now back to ~9.4x, down from ~13x in my last write-up. At 9.4x, it seems like the market is already pricing the potential near-term weakness from the tariffs and the fact that the AE brand still has areas of weakness. Moreover, on a relative basis, AEO is also not trading at an expensive level. AEO is now trading in line with peers over the past year, which I think is fair given the similar growth outlook (mid-single-digit revenue growth).

Conclusion

I am upgrading AEO to a hold rating. I believe AEO fundamentals have improved vs. when I last wrote about it, and I think that is most visible in Aerie’s strong growth and the early signs of stabilization at the AE brand. That said, I am not ready to turn bullish. The AE recovery story still needs more proof; the full-price sales trend remains an area to monitor; and tariff pressure could still weigh on earnings if mitigation falls short. At the current valuation, I think the stock now looks fairly priced.

data.title: CLOI: Buy The Complexity Premium Before It Disappears
data.description:
The VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI) is an actively managed ETF that is sub-advised by PineBridge Investments. The goal of the company is to invest primarily in IG-rated tranches of CLOs. Launched in June 2022, the fund has scaled to approximately $1.37 billion in net assets, establishing itself as one of the fastest-growing funds in the securitized credit space. The fund pays monthly distributions and benchmarks against the J.P. Morgan CLO IG Index, effective May of 2025.

CLOI's strategy allows investment across the IG CLO capital structure, from AAA through BBB, with up to 20% in BB-rated tranches. This flexibility is the fund's defining characteristic. Unlike JAAA, which is locked into the AAA tranche, PineBridge can dynamically allocate across the capital stack, moving up in quality during stress and reaching down when mezzanine spreads compensate for incremental risk. I am initiating CLOI as a buy on Seeking Alpha, as a persistent 50-150 bps complexity premium over comparable rated corporate credit, within a floating rate structure, effectively mitigates duration risk, with the active rotation providing a layer of alpha that static CLO allocations cannot replicate. While slightly higher risk, the returns justify a buy rating.

Top Holdings and Credit Quality

CLOI's portfolio is well-diversified across CLO managers and vintages. The most recent fact sheet has the following as their top holdings:

Holding Coupon Maturity % of Net Assets
U.S. Treasury Bill - 03/2026 7.65%
Anchorage Capital CLO 2026-22 Ltd 5.000% 01/2039 4.65%
Signal Peak CLO 4 Ltd 5.318% 10/2034 2.71%
Regatta VI Funding Ltd 5.206% 10/2038 2.67%
Ocean Trails CLO XV Ltd 4.932% 01/2039 2.57%

The 7.65% allocation to T-bills serves as a buffer to liquidity, which has become a standard practice for CLO ETFs that are actively managed, which partake in daily creation and redemption flows where the underlying securities often trade OTC. The quality of credit is largely within investment grade, with the fund historically holding approx. 95% of assets in IG-rated tranches. The top 10 holdings represent approximately 24.8% of net assets, which shows meaningful diversification across managers and assets.

The Reason for the Buy

As previously discussed in my analysis on JAAA, CLO coupons reset quarterly off SOFR, making duration risk negligible. CLOI's 0.02 beta confirms near-total decorrelation from equities, and the floating-rate structure means income adjusts with the rate environment rather than fighting it. Whether the Fed cuts twice or holds steady through 2026, CLOI's income stream recalibrates accordingly. The credit backdrop is supportive. Moody's projects U.S. speculative-grade defaults to decline to approximately 3.0% in 2026. The trailing twelve-month default rate within the Morningstar U.S. Leveraged Loan Index fell to 1.23% by the end of 2025. Lower defaults mean more consistent cash flows to IG tranches, less defense repositioning by CLO managers, and a higher yield. Importantly, 2025 CLO new issuance hit $201 billion, a record for the second consecutive year. This signals a robust institutional demand and deepening secondary market liquidity. Deutsche Bank Research notes the global CLO market now exceeds $1.5 trillion, yet ETF-accessible product remains a fraction of this total.

Due to CLOI having some subprime exposure, I would also like to mention that CLOs are not CDOs of 2008. CDOs were backed by static pools of subprime mortgage-backed securities with layered, opaque leverage (CDO-squared, complex SPVs, for instance) and no active management. CLOs are backed by diversified portfolios of senior secured corporate loans that are actively managed, with overcollateralization tests that force deleveraging before losses reach investment-grade tranches, which is why AAA CLO tranches have experienced zero realized defaults in the asset class's history, including through the GFC.

Competitor Comparisons

The CLO ETF universe now encompasses about 28 funds, with a combined AUM of approx. 42 billion. I have compared four of them below:

Metric JAAA CLOI JBBB CLOZ
Focus AAA Only IG (AAA-BBB) B-BBB BBB-B
AUM ~$27.2B ~$1.37B ~$1.1B ~$595M
Expense Ratio 0.20% 0.36% 0.47% 0.50%
Yield (Fwd) ~4.60% ~5.07% ~6.74% ~7.93%
3-Yr Ann. Return ~6.8% ~7.2% ~1.6% -
Max Drawdown ~-2.64% ~-4% ~-10.6% ~-5.3%

CLOI yields about 40-50 bps more than our safest option, JAAA, by reaching lower tranches such as A and BBB. The expense ratio is 16 bps higher, reflecting active management, but the net yield pickup compensates for this. CLOI has a 3-year annualized return of 7.2%, which exceeds JAAA's 6.8%. The tradeoff to this is a wider drawdown, as CLOI experiences an approx. 4% drawdown attributed to liquidity dynamics, but not credit deterioration, versus the -2.64% max drawdown of JAAA. As you start to venture down the tranches of JBBB and CLOZ, you experience a diminished risk-adjusted return due to the enhanced max drawdowns experienced from adventuring to lower-rated tranches. The yields may be higher, but there is materially higher drawdown risk, with JBBB having a maximum drawdown exceeding 10%. For a non-correlated sleeve that is designed to provide ballast during periods of equity stress, that drawdown profile defeats the purpose of having CLOs as an uncorrelated sleeve. CLOI provides a nice blend of risk-adjusted return for those seeking more yield than super prime CLO aggregates but without the drawdown risk of majority subprime CLO aggregates.

Tradability

CLOI has an AUM of 1.37B and has an average daily volume of approx. 220,000 shares. For retail and even institutional positioning, this results in trading without much impact. For block orders, initiators can access a creation/redemption mechanism, which provides further liquidity beyond what the volume on the tape suggests. Bid-ask spreads typically run 2-10 bps, or about 1-5 cents. This is relatively tight for a securitized credit ETF and reflects the liquidity of IG CLO tranches and active market making. The fund typically also trades within a few cents of their NAV, with market stress incidents resolving relatively quickly. Monthly distributions also provide a semi-predictable income pattern for liability-matching or income-oriented investors, depending on the overall strategy of the portfolio and the use of structured credit within.

Positive Catalysts

Fed rate cuts over the previous seven months and an additional cut expected this year will further compress money market yields and will potentially drive flows into CLO ETFs as the next alternative. This is starting to become apparent, with $301 million of net inflows over the trailing year into CLOI. Additionally, declining default rates, alongside record CLO issuances, deepen the secondary market and tighten the trading cost of CLOI and adjacent. PineBridge actively rotates into cheaper mezzanine tranches during opportunities presenting themselves amidst volatility, as shown during their Q4 2025 commentary, noting a preference for higher-rated tranches with a willingness and readiness to shift down to lower tranches when value opportunities emerge.

Downside Risks

An emerging downside risk involves the Fed compressing SOFR beneath 3% by cutting rates, which will mechanically reduce the coupon income by 100 bps or more. Although, this would also signal an environment that is favorable to credit quality. Tariff escalation and additional geopolitical shocks could also potentially push leveraged loan defaults of about 5%, breaching CLO overcollateralization tests and diverting cash flows away from mezzanine tranches. CLO ETFs can also experience liquidity mismatches during stress, forcing trading prices at a discount to NAV, as seen in 2023. While the issue in 2023 resolved quickly, it can underscore the risks of wrapping OTC credit in a daily, freely traded ETF. Covenant erosion in leveraged lending is also a risk, driven by competition between broadly syndicated lenders and private credit, reducing recovery rates in the future (even if the headline default rates are contained).

Conclusion and Takeaway

CLOI harvests the complexity premium of CLOs at IG credit quality. The active management of the fund adds a layer of alpha over passive alternatives, and the IG strategy provides a natural ceiling on the credit risk of the fund that more aggressive CLO ETFs tend to lack. CLOI slightly outperformed the JPM CLO IG Benchmark on both a quarterly and 1Y basis, which demonstrates a value-add by PineBridge beyond their 0.36% management fee. For an investor who is building a non-correlated income sleeve or seeking a higher-yielding replacement for static super prime CLO ETFs as the Fed normalizes rates, CLOI earns a buy.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap - Week Ending April 02, 2026

U.S. equity markets continued their upward momentum over the past week, with broad-based gains across major sectors led by technology, industrials, and small-cap stocks. Growth-oriented ETFs, such as the NASDAQ 100 and Technology Select Sector, stood out with strong advances that reflect renewed investor confidence in innovation-driven segments. Meanwhile, financials, materials, and communication services also posted solid gains, signaling a constructive risk-on environment. The notable exception was the energy sector, which faced pressure amid declining oil prices. This highlights a divergence in sector performance as markets re-calibrate expectations around global demand and macroeconomic trends.

In the below tables, we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, and finally, our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

After a disastrous March for all the major foreign and domestic indexed ETFs invested in broad market indexes, US investors bought the dip last week. Nevertheless, we expect that most investors will see that as a “dead cat bounce” until more clarity is brought to the global geopolitical situation. For ValuEngine, the best strategy we can promote is to stay the course in using data-driven tools for investment decisions, not fears of wars raging out of control while the US struggles to find an exit strategy.

The good news is that our models now consider just 52% of the stocks we follow to be overvalued, as compared with more than 65% six months ago. Stocks recently upgraded to 4 (Buy) include: natural resources provider Cadiz Inc. (CDZI); BioCryst Pharmaceuticals Inc., (BCRX), a leader in the use of crystallography and structure-based drug design; Gilead Sciences (GILD), a leader in HIV and other immunodeficiency treatments; and hardware supplier Fastenal (FAST). Although we do not currently recommend large-cap health care as shown by our 2 (Sell) rating on the State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV), there are a number of biotech stocks that are recommended now for consideration for potential purchase.

Two new ValuEngine downgrades are in two of the industries most affected by the increases in fuel prices and global fighting. Airline JetBlue (JBLU) has been downgraded to 2 (Sell), and logistics provider RXO Inc. (RXO) has also been downgraded to 2. These companies will be challenged to maintain positive earnings streams until fuel prices stabilize and shipping businesses return to something resembling normalcy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Olin: Improving Margins By Factoring Cost Savings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

During the Investor Day of 2024, Olin Corporation (OLN) set a bold milestone of reaching roughly $250 million in cost savings by 2029. This comes as the firm tries to bring in capital and operational efficiencies to counter the weak demand for its EDC and related products due to low construction activities that we have been witnessing for almost a decade. Thus far, Olin is succeeding in bringing in these cost savings. For the full year of 2025, the company managed to reduce costs by $44 million, and in 2026, it estimates more than $100 million of annual cost savings. If this estimate gets hit, more margin growth is to be expected, which will enable the firm to finance debt repayments, continue to pay dividends, and put itself in a great position to navigate these trough seasons without risking liquidity. Analyzing its 2025 financials, Olin shows better growth and profitability metrics than its industry peers while trading at a massive discount. Making me keen to rate it a buy.

Momentum Factor

Over the past 3 years, Olin has been an underperformer compared to its peers and the broader market. Most of this underperformance can be attributed to having a lower market cap, high debt levels, and weak demand for PVC products, as the construction industry has been largely depressed in recent years. In this 3-year period, Olin lost about 45% of its value, while Westlake (WLK) and the S&P 500 gained roughly 11% and 67%, respectively.

The scatterplot below shows that in the last 3 years, most names in this industry got hit pretty bad in price action. But we have started to see a price recovery YTD, with only 3 names on the negative y-axis. Dow (DOW) and Westlake scored an A and an A- for momentum, as they returned roughly 77% and 62% YTD, respectively. Olin returned only 37% and received a B rating. Momentum has improved just recently on this name because YoY, Olin returned 23% while its sector returned 36%, but in the last 3 months, it returned 40.37% vs. the sector return of just 6.53%. What this tells me is that more investors are starting to turn bullish, and if management successfully executes on cost savings, more buyers are to be expected as current multiples haven't priced in these improving fundamentals.

Management has taken advantage of this undervaluation to repurchase some of Olin's shares. In 2022, they executed the largest repurchase at a north of $1.3 billion, followed by a $700 million buyback in 2023. Last year, the number of shares that were bought back were valued at $51 million. In aggregate, the firm managed to reduce share count to 113.86 million.

Financial Analysis

Top line performance for Olin and its peers has been in a decline since 2023, and the peak growth decline for Olin hit 25% during 2024 on a trailing basis, the worst growth decline vs. peers. During the second half of 2025, the growth rate has flipped positive while peers remain in the negative territory, showing improving product mix and demand recovery in other segments of Olin's business. Adding the massive cost savings expected to be realized this year to this growth recovery will be a significant catalyst for margin expansion going forward.

Looking at Olin's revenue by segments, we can see that vs. 3 years ago, the top line got hit pretty bad across most segments, but there's some stability and growth on a YoY basis. CAPV revenue was at $5.09 billion in 2022; that revenue slid to $3.63 billion in 2024 and has since recovered by 1.38% to $3.68 billion in 2025. Epoxy revenue declined from $2.7 billion in 2022 to $1.23 billion in 2024 and has since recovered by 11.38% to $1.37 billion in 2025. Looking at the Winchester segment, we can see that the commercial side continued to witness revenue declines while the military has been trending upwards sequentially. This is due to low-volume selling and inventory reduction from the commercial side and high demand from the military side. Even the margin side of Winchester got hit by high input costs from copper and brass prices; adding to this margin contraction was high power cost in the fourth quarter due to cold weather conditions.

FY2025 Income Statement

Full year revenue increased by 4% to $6.78 million, with the epoxy segment leading growth by 12%, followed by the Winchester segment at 2% and the CAPV segment at 1%. The recovery in the epoxy segment mainly came from anti-dumping policies that intend to reduce low-quality and low-priced Asian exports to the European and North American regions. Due to the factors I stated above, the cost of revenue increased 8% to $6.28 billion, which led to gross profits decreasing 32% to $501.5 million and landing gross margins at 7.40%. Operating expenses increased 13% to $496.2 million, with $32.9 million coming from one-time restructuring costs, resulting in operating income decreasing 98% to $5.3 million and a pretax loss of $161.1 million.

FY2025 Cash Flow Statement

Net loss increased 196% to $101.1 million, but operating cash flow was positive at $474.2 million. What contributed to the positive cash flow was D&A, which increased moderately at 1%, and a positive working capital of $157.9 million. Free cash flow decreased 20% to $227.2 million, offset by capital expenditures of $226.3 million, which increased 16% YoY. Guiding for 2026, management expects capital spending, D&A, and pension income to be lower than the 2025 levels. Interest and restructuring expenses are expected to be at the same level. Factoring in the expectation of a cash-free tax year, FCF margins should be more than the current levels going forward, which should translate to more buybacks, debt repayments, and continued dividend payouts.

FY2025 Balance Sheet

While cash reserves decreased 5% to $167.6 million, current debt decreased 15% to $109.7 million, and long-term debt was flat at $2.72 million. A significant amount of debt maturities will be settled in 2030, valued at $1.04 billion, but around $370 million will need to be settled in 2027. This does raise some concerns as to how the company will be able to increase its cash levels while having to also pay dividends and interest expenses. Something has to give, and my gut tells me, share repurchases. I do feel like the firm might be cornered to pause buybacks to create enough liquidity room for itself. The firm has purchased a lot of shares already, so I see this as a minor setback to shareholder return.

Quantitative Analysis

Growth Factor

Starting with the growth factor, Olin is one of the few names in its industry that shows a positive YoY revenue growth; its closest peers are in line with the trend line in the third quadrant, showing 3 years and YoY of revenue decline. The growth rating has been upgraded to a D-, while Dow is still with a rating of F and Westlake with a rating of D. Olin grew revenue by 4% YoY, in line with its sector growth, while Dow and Westlake shrank revenue by 7% and 8%, respectively.

Profitability Factor

Olin also shows better profitability metrics than its peers. Even though this chart shows that most industry peers have a higher EBIT and FCF margin (they are in the first quadrant), Dow and Westlake are not found there and instead have a negative EBIT margin. Olin still got a C+ rating for profitability, and this was largely bolstered by the FCF margin of 5.50% vs. the sector margin of 4.82%, but other than that, broader margins still massively trail its sector median.

On an annual basis, we can see that FCF margins have been contracting significantly, but unlike its peers, Olin's margins remain positive, and given the factors I stated above, I expect these margins to trend even higher in the second half of 2026.

Valuation Factor

Despite showing better growth and profitability metrics, Olin remains trading at a discount to its peers. Even though the valuation looks fair on an EBITDA multiple (9.55 vs. sector median of 10.91), price to cash flow is significantly discounted, with an upside potential of 68% (6.85 vs. sector median of 11.79). Seeing Olin positioned below the trendline in the scatterplot below is illustrative of this discount.

Risk

While writing this article, I was intending to rate Olin a strong buy, but the debt levels made me question whether a strong buy rating is deserved. I don't think it does; even though maturities have been pushed years away, interest expenses are really hurting FCF and net margins. The chart below shows that Olin has one of the lowest interest coverage ratios while also showing a higher debt-to-equity ratio amongst its industry peers. With the current debt level, Olin would have to flawlessly execute these restructuring efforts and make sure that costs don't balloon beyond interest coverage, or else it could be forced to take on even more debt. Given that the firm is still tracing well on its cost-saving goals, this does alleviate debt concerns a little bit.

Conclusion

Olin has recently improved its top line growth, and with the massive cost savings expected to be added this year, I estimate FCF margins will continue to be positive or even trend higher than current levels. This makes Olin one of the few firms in its industry with the operational excellence to continue issuing dividend payouts and repay debt while navigating the tough environment that has been persisting for years. Growth, momentum, and profitability metrics look promising, but the firm remains undervalued relative to its peers and sector, giving us upside potential of 68% if multiples were to re-rate higher. For this reason, I rate Olin a buy.

data.title: Western Union: 11% Yield Is A Buy Signal On Margin Strength
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Western Union (WU) is one of the stocks I've covered most frequently on Seeking Alpha in recent years, with its chronic undervaluation appearing somewhat detached from operational realities, in my view.

A month and a half on from what I would suggest were pretty reasonable Q4 results, the firm continues to trade at a significant discount, with the dividend yield approaching a very generous 11% as a result.

As I've highlighted previously, valuation multiples applied to the firm (such as an adjusted P/E of ~5x) would usually be reflective of a business heading for bankruptcy. While the remittance industry faces headwinds, Western Union's core operations remain robust. With the stock down around 15% since early February, I would argue that now is a good opportunity to buy the stock.

Q4 Margin Strength

I wasn't able to publish an earnings review for the firm's fourth quarter and full-year report released in late February, though it is worth perhaps offering some brief comments on those figures in order to contextualize the rest of my up-to-date analysis on the firm.

In my last article on Western Union, I said that "if margins are stable or improved for the 4Q25, my bullish thesis is basically intact". Therefore, it was very encouraging to see the 20% Q4 adjusted margin at the same level as in the previous quarter. The more striking comparison, however, is with the fourth quarter last year, when adjusted margins were only 17%. This would suggest that the firm's previously outlined cost-saving initiatives, such as making KYC processes more efficient, have been highly effective.

With the remittance industry (and particularly the agent model used by Western Union) in somewhat of a secular decline, it was not surprising to see revenues down by 5% in Q4. I had warned of "a revenue decline of somewhere between 2-5% in consideration of enforcement intensification in the U.S. market" in my previous article, though clearly the results ultimately released came in toward the heavier end of my prediction.

Overall, though, the Q4 results were basically in line with my expectations, and the most encouraging aspect for me was the continued strengthening of the firm's margins, which are essential given the ongoing pressure on revenues.

Migration Policies

As I've previously argued, migration policy around the world (and particularly in the United States) has a significant long-term impact on Western Union and the broader remittance sector, given that the industry traditionally relies on migrant workers sending back a portion of their earnings to families in their countries of origin. Western Union's management team is acutely aware of the potential long-term impacts of migration policy, with CFO Matthew Cagwin recently highlighting that "we've seen immigration to the U.S. [...] dropped by about two-thirds from pre-Trump to where we are now".

Immigration policy was the overwhelming focus of my previous article, where I discussed the indefinite 75-country U.S. immigrant visa ban, which is still in effect. It is important to note, though, that a legal challenge to this ban (CLINIC v. Rubio) is now making its way through the courts. On March 10th, the plaintiffs in the case filed a Motion for Partial Summary Judgment, which might result in the ban being lifted more rapidly, though it is difficult to estimate the likelihood of this ultimately taking place. If the ban were ultimately lifted, it would certainly reduce some of the long-term revenue pressure in the North America region, which relies on the base of remitters being replenished with new arrivals over time.

As I've previously stated, it's easy to focus exclusively on the United States when thinking about outgoing remittances, but the recently filed 10-K covering the 2025 financial year shows that only 35.7% of revenue is attributed to the North American region. It also showed an absolute increase of around 5.8%, from ~$934 million to ~$998 million, in revenue generated from the Europe and CIS region, highlighting the increasing importance of this region for the firm as U.S. revenue softens due to the current administration's policies.

An important reason why I continue to be bullish on Western Union is that, while the U.S. is a tough market for the firm at the moment, immigration policies in other nations are not necessarily as hostile to the firm's operations. One good example of this is the recent announcement from the Spanish government of a new wave of regularization for illegal immigrants already in the country. Given that this will include anybody who has lived in Spain since 31st July of last year, it will cover a very large number of people and is quite likely to represent a pull factor for further migration to the European Union. According to Eurostat, Spain saw 1,288,600 immigrants in 2024, representing a large addressable market for Western Union. These migrants will, in some cases, now be sending larger amounts as they are given legal status and emerge from the "shadow" economy in Spain.

Geopolitical Impacts

At the moment, most public companies are being impacted to some degree by the conflict in Iran, and Western Union is no exception.

Interestingly, with the Middle East being a key source for outbound money transfers, the Iran conflict in the short term has been relatively positive for Western Union. CFO Matthew Cagwin also commented on this, noting that "typically, when there is a conflict, we will see people wanting to get their money out of the conflict zone". This is particularly relevant for the UAE, which has been noted by the firm as an important outbound remittance source. I would suggest that this is likely to bolster the firm's Q1 results somewhat.

Longer term, I think it's important to put the context into perspective. One could draw a parallel with the Russia-Ukraine conflict. In that worst-case scenario where Western Union had to totally suspend its operations in Russia, the long-term impacts of that are not particularly visible today, and there is not really the prospect of Western Union suspending its operations in any Middle Eastern countries at the moment in the near future, as far as I can tell.

The other thing to note is that, unlike many businesses, the fact that Western Union is a relatively capital-light operation means that it is unlikely to be affected by any of the direct commodity price or shipping-related considerations in the same way as traditional retail or commodity businesses, for example. Western Union is driven by migrants sending money back home to their families, which is relatively resistant to geopolitical considerations such as we are seeing in Iran. The indirect effect of worsening economic conditions everywhere would likely impact remittance volumes, but this sort of broad observation can be applied to most public companies, I would suggest. Overall, then, I am not overly concerned about the conflict as it relates to Western Union.

Operational Considerations

While "macro" and broad sector considerations are important, we also got a sense of what's going on operationally for Western Union when CFO Matthew Cagwin spoke at the Wolfe Research FinTech Forum on March 10th.

One interesting point related to the acquisition of Intermex, where Cagwin gave a sense of the management's motivations behind that purchase. He commented that in spite of Intermex's "very antiquated digital platform" they had managed to achieve 6% penetration (versus in-person retail). I would argue that there is an opportunity for further margin improvement by making Intermex's operations more efficient, potentially cutting down on expensive retail locations and migrating that business over to Western Union's digital platform.

I also got a similar sense of this quest for margin improvement in Cagwin's comments on the firm's new stablecoin, Digital Asset Network. The firm's intention is to "pull a lot of the capital out of the marketplace through using a digital asset" and this may "save the commission expense because you didn't have to go through an agent to pay it out". If executed properly, this will be fantastic for the company, though I am not totally convinced by the strategy, as I will explore in the "Risks" section below.

Risks to Thesis

There are a couple of factors worth considering that might represent a threat to my bullish thesis on Western Union. The first category is broader considerations such as migration policy and geopolitical considerations, which we have already discussed in some detail.

The second risk that investors should pay close attention to is the risk of an aggressive move over to the firm's digital platform and Digital Asset Network cannibalizing the agent network, which represents an important competitive advantage for Western Union versus its competitors.

As stated above by Cagwin, part of the motivation for the Digital Asset Network is reducing the costs associated with the agent network. The treasury side to the network is also likely to reduce fees paid to agents and correspondent banks to ensure that there is liquidity available for remittance payouts at all times.

While this can be mostly seen as a positive cost-saving initiative, the big risk is that Western Union "bets the farm" on a successful move over to almost exclusively using its digital platform while losing physical agents (particularly independent ones) by not paying them enough. The company does need to ensure a balanced approach, in my view, and needs to recognize that other players in the digital remittance space, such as Remitly (RELY), already have effective (and "sticky") digital remittance offerings. Since the firm's massive agent network is a profitable component of its business, I would argue that it is important that it not be done away with too aggressively.

Valuation

While there are risks for Western Union, I do think the margin of safety offered by the extremely low earnings multiple does mitigate my concerns about these to some degree. As I have hinted at, it is rare to see a (non-commodity) business trading at a P/E ratio of 5x without major concerns about its balance sheet or some other exceptional situation. Western Union's low earnings multiple alongside relatively stable and predictable quarterly earnings does make it highly unusual.

Also notable is the fact that intraday price swings over the past couple of days have pushed the firm's dividend yield very close to 11%. I think this is a clear case of being paid to wait for the firm's price to re-rate as the market eventually recognizes that the extreme investor pessimism surrounding the firm doesn't really align with operational reality.

Q1 Earnings Preview

Before concluding, it is worth just highlighting that Western Union is due to announce its Q1 results on 23rd April.

I expect normalized earnings to come in at around the consensus estimate of $0.40. This compares with $0.41 for the first quarter last year and $0.42, $0.47, and $0.45 in Q2, Q3, and Q4, respectively.

I am expecting a slightly worse result than the same time last year because Q1 is the first quarter where we are likely to see some of the impacts of the 75-country ban (introduced Jan 21st), and it's fair to say that the enforcement activity has broadly intensified in the U.S. between 1Q25 and 1Q26. At the same time, I do not expect earnings to be terrible, partially because of the artificial boost in Middle East outflows resulting from the Iran conflict but also because of the long-term secular strength in the firm's Europe region.

Conclusion

While the agent-oriented remittance business faces headwinds, it also represents an important component of a globalized economy in which migrant workers are an essential ingredient. U.S. immigration policy has had a tangible impact on the firm's results, but at the same time, it remains robustly profitable, with 20% adjusted margins in the last quarter. The firm's 5x earnings multiple also represents a significant margin of safety for investors.

Overall, then, I maintain my buy rating and believe that Western Union's stock remains an attractive opportunity.

data.title: The Brink's Company: Potential To Be A High Earnings Compounder
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on The Brink’s Company (BCO) was a buy rating because the growth visibility has gotten clearer with AMS/DRS ramping up nicely and the valuation was still reasonable. I reiterate my buy rating as the recurring-revenue mix is getting larger and is now meaningful enough to move the needle, while NATL adds a second growth leg that could broaden BCO’s service offering and lift margin potential if executed well. At around 11x forward PE, I do not think the stock reflects this setup, especially if BCO becomes a stronger earnings compounder over time.

The recurring-revenue thesis is ramping nicely

My core view on BCO was that AMS and DRS will become a bigger part of the business over time, and that should lift both growth quality and margins. Recall earlier, AMS and DRS were growing 16% organically and made up ~25% of total revenue on a trailing twelve months [TTM] basis. Those figures have accelerated to 22% organic growth, and the mix is now 28% of TTM revenue. The momentum is apparently not stopping. Management is guiding mid-to-high teens organic growth for AMS/DRS in 2026, with a mix moving to 30% to 32%. As the mix becomes larger, I don’t think the market can continue to discount this part of the business as "A small part of BCO." When a revenue stream moves from about one-quarter of the base toward one-third and is still growing at mid-teens organically, the contribution shifts from low single digits to mid-single digits.

While the traditional CVM was flat organically, and overall blended organic growth came in at mid-single-digit, if we assume traditional CVM stays flat and AMS/DRS becomes, say, 80% of the business, there is no reason to believe the headline revenue growth will not accelerate.

NATL

A new growth catalyst to point out is NCR Atleos [NATL]. BCO agreed to acquire NATL for $6.6 billion, made up of 13.3 million BCO shares, $2.2 billion in cash, and the assumption of $2.6 billion of NATL debt. Management's view on this acquisition is that it would broaden BCO’s service offering and better connect AMS, DRS, and traditional CVM. They are also targeting $200 million of annual run-rate cost synergies within three years of closing, with the deal expected to be at least 35% accretive to EPS and to close in 1Q27.

For context, NATL is a self-service banking and ATM infrastructure business. It is a leading ATMaaS and managed services provider, one of the largest independently owned and operated ATM networks globally, and also a leading provider of multi-vendor ATM software applications and middleware. Most importantly, this is not a start-up. NATL already has a large installed base, including owned and operated ATMs, and this is why I believe it fits well into the BCO ecosystem. BCO already had a strong position in the cash, service, and route sides of ATM operations through AMS. With NATL, BCO is moving further up the value chain, whereby it is getting closer to controlling more of the ATM ownership model end to end.

This is a really interesting point to note because NATL adds a real growth leg to BCO’s growth story. Here is what I expect to happen. For banks, BCO’s pitch will become stronger as it can now offer a more integrated solution that can lower the cost to serve and simplify ATM ownership through a single-vendor setup. As for retailers, I think the appeal is pretty straightforward. BCO should be able to streamline more of the cash and ATM workflow inside stores because BCO now controls more of the stack (software, hardware, monitoring, processing, cash forecasting, replenishment, and field service). That should improve routing and scheduling as well, since one platform can match all the retailer needs, it makes sense for retailers to just stay with BCO as it lowers complexity, has better uptime, and lessens the need to manage multiple vendors.

As such, this NATL catalyst will add on to the already strong fundamentals of BCO. If the deal closes and integration goes well, BCO could end up with more recurring and subscription-like revenue, a wider solution set, and more room for margin improvement than what I already thought.

Valuation

BCO’s overall FY2025 revenue growth and earnings were pretty much in line with my expectations. Yes, adj. earnings margin was a miss, but at the current valuation of 11x forward PE, the stock has turned cheap again. In my model, I now expect growth to accelerate to low-teen percentages in FY2028, assuming the NATL deal comes through. Margin-wise, I expect BCO margins to go even higher as BCO should achieve both its revenue and cost synergies with NATL. I would assume multiples will go up if BCO becomes a >20% earnings compounder, but even if I assume 11x NTM PE (the multiple today), the stock is still worth 55% more.

Risk

The main risk now is the NATL execution. If regulatory approval takes longer, integration is not well done, or the $200 million synergy target falls short, the market may turn a lot more negative on this deal. And this matters because my growth acceleration and margin expansion assumption relies partially on this deal, not just on AMS/DRS.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on BCO. The AMS/DRS side of the story continues to work. But NATL adds a new path for BCO to deepen customer relationships and potentially accelerate growth and margins beyond what I had initially thought. I see execution risk around the deal, but I am hopeful it will turn out well. At the current valuation, I believe the upside remains attractive.
